grammar: "Buenos" agrees with the masculine plural noun "días." Spanish greetings change form: buenos días, buenas tardes, buenas noches.
"Lo" is a neuter pronoun meaning "it." "Siento" comes from sentir (to feel). Literally: "I feel it."
"Estar" is used for temporary states (how you feel right now). Compare with "ser" for permanent traits. ¿Cómo estás? = How are you feeling? vs. ¿Cómo eres? = What are you like?
"Estoy" is the yo (I) form of estar. Used here because feeling fine is a temporary state.
Negation in Spanish: just put "no" before the verb. No entiendo, no hablo, no sé.
Reflexive verb: llamarse (to call oneself). "Te llamas" = you call yourself. Literally: "How do you call yourself?"
"Me llamo" = I call myself. The reflexive pronoun "me" refers back to the subject (yo). This is how Spanish states names.
"Ser" is used for origin, identity, and permanent traits. Soy = I am (permanent). "Soy de" = I am from.
No auxiliary verb needed for questions in Spanish! Just raise your intonation. "Hablas inglés" (you speak English) → "¿Hablas inglés?" (do you speak English?).
"Poder" (can/to be able) + infinitive is how Spanish says "can you do X?" Puedes = you can. Puedo = I can.
"Estar" is used for location. Está = it is (located). "Dónde" always carries an accent when it's a question word.
"Ser" is used for identity and profession. Note: no article needed — "soy estudiante" not "soy un estudiante" (though both work).
Gender agreement: amigo (male friend) → amiga (female friend). Most nouns ending in -o are masculine, -a are feminine.
Ser conjugation: soy (I), eres (you), es (he/she), somos (we), sois (you all), son (they). Subject pronouns (yo, tú, él...) are often dropped.
"Un" is the indefinite article for masculine nouns (a/an). Un libro (a book), una mesa (a table). "El/la" are the definite articles (the).

english: I don't speak Spanish very well.

grammar: "Tener" (to have) is irregular: tengo (I have), tienes (you have), tiene (he/she has). It's also used for age, hunger, thirst, and more.
"Hay" means "there is" or "there are" — it's the same for singular and plural. Hay un gato. Hay tres gatos.
"Me" is the indirect object pronoun (to me). It can go before the conjugated verb: "me puedes ayudar" = "puedes ayudarme." Both are correct.
Adjectives agree with the subject: cansado (m), cansada (f). A woman would say "estoy cansada." Estar is used because tiredness is temporary.
Spanish uses "tener" (to have) not "estar" (to be) for hunger, thirst, cold, heat, fear, and age. Literally: "I have hunger."

english: What do you want to do?
grammar: "Querer" (to want) + infinitive: quiero comer (I want to eat), quieres hacer (you want to do). Querer is stem-changing: e→ie.
"Al" is a contraction of "a" + "el" (to the). Spanish has two mandatory contractions: al (a + el) and del (de + el).
"Bueno" shortens to "buen" before a masculine singular noun. Similarly: malo → mal, primero → primer, tercero → tercer.
Regular -ir verb: vivir → vivo, vives, vive, vivimos, vivís, viven. The three verb families end in -ar, -er, -ir.

english: I live in a big city.
grammar: Adjective placement: most adjectives go after the noun in Spanish. "Ciudad grande" (big city), not "grande ciudad."
"Cuál" vs "qué": use "cuál" when choosing from options (which one?), "qué" for definitions (what is X?). ¿Cuál es tu nombre? vs ¿Qué es esto?
Present continuous: estar + gerund (-ando/-iendo). Estoy aprendiendo = I am learning. Estoy comiendo = I am eating.

english: I don't know where it is.
grammar: "Saber" (to know facts) vs "conocer" (to know/be familiar with). Sé la respuesta (I know the answer). Conozco Madrid (I know Madrid).
"Vamos" means both "we go" and "let's go!" It's one of the few verbs where the indicative doubles as a command.

english: What do you do for a living?

grammar: Age uses "tener" (to have), not "ser" (to be). Literally: "How many years do you have?" Tengo 25 años = I am 25 years old.

english: Can you write it down, please?
Is there someone who speaks English?
grammar: Subjunctive preview! "Hable" (not "habla") because the person is hypothetical — you don't know if they exist. This is the subjunctive mood.

english: I need to find my hotel.

grammar: "Gustar" works backwards from English: the thing you like is the subject. "Me gusta la ciudad" = the city pleases me. "Me gustan los gatos" = cats please me (plural).

english: How long have you been here?
grammar: "Llevar" + time = to have been (doing something) for (duration). Llevo dos años aquí = I've been here for two years. Very common construction.

target: Llevo una semana en esta ciudad.
english: I've been in this city for a week.
Do you know where there's a pharmacy?
Shall we meet tomorrow at ten?
Would you like to have a coffee?
grammar: "Gustaría" is the conditional of gustar — would like. "Te gustaría" = it would please you = would you like. Politer than "¿quieres?"

target: Está a dos calles de aquí.
Necesito comprar un mapa de la ciudad.
english: I need to buy a map of the city.
Can you recommend a good place?
I want to see the tourist attractions.
How much is the entrance to the museum?

target: Muchas gracias por toda tu ayuda.
english: Thank you very much for all your help.

target: Ha sido un placer hablar contigo.
english: It's been a pleasure talking to you.
grammar: "Ha sido" = present perfect of ser (has been). Formed with haber + past participle. "Contigo" is a special form meaning "with you" (con + ti → contigo).

english: I hope to see you again soon.
grammar: "Esperar" + infinitive = to hope to. The pronoun attaches to the infinitive: ver + te = verte (to see you).
"Cuídate" is an informal command (tú imperative) of the reflexive verb cuidarse. The reflexive pronoun "-te" attaches to the end of imperative verbs.
